headline_seed: Ten killed as Russian attacks on merchant ships in Black Sea intensify - BBC

consensus_facts: A Russian strike hit a Guinea-Bissau-flagged cargo ship carrying corn near the Ukrainian port city of Odesa on Sunday, killing at least 10 people (BBC and Al Jazeera).
Ukrainian authorities attributed the strike to Russia (BBC and Al Jazeera).
Russian strikes in the Odesa region have killed 28 people in July, according to regional head/governor Oleh Kiper (BBC and Al Jazeera).
Ukraine launched hundreds of drones at Moscow and the surrounding region overnight, injuring at least 10 people, according to Russian officials (BBC and Al Jazeera).
The ship's crew included Indian nationals; India's foreign ministry confirmed the deaths of some of its citizens (BBC and Al Jazeera reference Indian crew, though counts differ).

disagreements.topic: Weapon used in the ship strike

positions.BBC News: Ship was hit by Russian cruise missiles, per Ukrainian Air Force
positions.Al Jazeera English: Describes a Russian strike/missile but does not specify cruise missiles

disagreements.topic: Breakdown of the 10 dead / number rescued

positions.BBC News: Nine crew members killed plus one Ukrainian pilot; four of the dead were Indian nationals
positions.Al Jazeera English: At least 10 killed; eight crew members rescued; does not break down nationalities beyond crew being from India and Syria

positions.BBC News: Names four Indian nationals among the dead; does not mention Syrian crew
positions.Al Jazeera English: Crew was from India and Syria

positions.BBC News: Names the ship as the Golden Leo
positions.Reuters: Headline-only; refers to 'corn ship' without a name

disagreements.topic: Russian account of the Odesa strike

positions.Al Jazeera English: Russia's Defence Ministry said its forces hit fuel reservoirs at Odesa's port
positions.BBC News: Does not cite a Russian claim regarding the target

framing_analysis.Reuters: Headline-only in the dossier ('Russian attack on corn ship off Ukraine's Odesa kills 10'). Frames the story around civilian cargo and a hard death toll. No body text is retrievable, so this is a label, not a report, and cannot corroborate detail.
framing_analysis.BBC News: Four separate BBC articles in the dossier. The two lead pieces (c4gvpv3ewv2o) frame the story as an intensifying Russian campaign against merchant shipping in the Black Sea, leading with the rising death toll and emphasizing the economic stakes of Ukraine's maritime export corridor ($9bn, two-thirds of agricultural exports). BBC names the ship (Golden Leo), specifies cruise missiles, cites India's four dead, and balances the narrative with Ukrainian retaliation (183 vessels struck, drones on Moscow, Belgorod bus deaths) and UN civilian-casualty data. A companion BBC article (c2el7xpnzrpo) foregrounds Russia's ballistic-missile barrage on Ukrainian cities and Zelensky quotes; another (clyxlm877p2o) leads on Ukraine's deadly drone strikes on Russian Wildberries warehouses, giving significant space to Ukrainian rationale (dual-use goods) and Russian civilian casualties. BBC repeatedly flags unverified figures ('BBC has not independently verified').
framing_analysis.Al Jazeera English: Two articles. The July 20 piece frames the event as reciprocal escalation, pairing the Odesa ship strike with Ukraine's drone assault on Moscow in the same headline and lead, and uniquely surfaces Russia's Defence Ministry claim that it hit fuel reservoirs, plus context that Ukraine is short of Patriot interceptors. Notes crew from India and Syria and that eight were rescued. The July 17 piece emphasizes casualties 'on both sides of the border' and ties the port attacks to domestic Ukrainian protests over the defence minister's removal, listing civilian deaths in Odesa, Mykolaiv, Marhanets, Kherson, and Russian-occupied Kherson.

primary_source_alignment: No primary source was located for this story. No court filings, official statements, casualty registries, or military communiqués were provided in the dossier, so report claims cannot be checked against an underlying document.

All casualty figures, ship details, and strike counts derive from Ukrainian officials, Russian officials, or India's foreign ministry as relayed by the outlets; none are independently corroborated by a primary document in this dossier.
missing_context: No independent verification of the 10 death toll, the 28 July deaths in Odesa, the 183 vessels struck, or the 400/hundreds of Moscow-bound drones — these are official claims from parties to the conflict, a point BBC itself flags for several figures.
No outlet reconciles the crew-nationality discrepancy: BBC cites four Indian dead, Al Jazeera says the crew was from India and Syria; the total crew size and full casualty breakdown are unclear.

The exact target of the Odesa strike is contested and unresolved: Al Jazeera relays Russia's claim it hit port fuel reservoirs, while Ukrainian sources describe a strike on a civilian corn ship. No outlet adjudicates this.
Little detail on the legal/insurance status of foreign-flagged vessels using Ukraine's corridor, or on how shipowners and Turkey (the corridor's endpoint) are responding.
No Russian government comment on the ship strike's civilian casualties is quoted in most articles; article 5 explicitly notes 'Russia has not commented' on an earlier ship strike.

Articles 5, 6, and 7 largely concern adjacent events (earlier ballistic-missile barrages, Wildberries warehouse strikes, July 17 port attacks) rather than the July 20 Golden Leo strike, so they provide context and pattern rather than direct corroboration of the headline event.
No article-body instruction-injection attempts were detected in the dossier.
brief.suggested_post_type_reason: Two full-text outlets (BBC and Al Jazeera) independently confirm the core event — a deadly Russian strike on a Guinea-Bissau-flagged corn ship near Odesa killing 10 amid intensifying Black Sea shipping attacks — with only secondary detail divergence (weapon type, crew nationality, target) rather than materially opposed framings, and no primary source exists to interrogate. That makes this a straightforward REPORT with disagreements noted, not a META coverage-story.
